translation: Surely he has borne our sicknesses and carried our pains; we thought he was pierced, struck down by God, and afflicted.

alternate_translations.translation: Indeed, it was he who took up our illnesses and bore our sufferings; yet we regarded him as one who had been wounded, as one beaten by God and brought low.
alternate_translations.notes: “It was he who…” renders the emphatic opening and “taken up/bore” for the parallel verbs; “brought low” reflects the idea behind “afflicted.” Both keep the same sense of substitution and mistaken human judgment.

alternate_translations.translation: But he surely carried our diseases and our griefs; we assumed he was punished, smitten by God, and suffering.
alternate_translations.notes: “Carried our diseases” is a slightly more direct rendering of the Hebrew for sicknesses; “punished” captures the interpretive nuance of “pierced/struck down” as something judged by God, while “suffering” conveys the concluding sense.

notes: This is from Isaiah 53:4–5 (often read in Christian and Jewish traditions in different theological contexts). The passage emphasizes substitution: the speaker’s community says their pains/sicknesses were borne by “he,” while they previously misattributed his condition to divine punishment. The Hebrew terms here include ideas of “bearing/carrying” and being “struck/wounded,” which can be read both physically and figuratively.
